sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Factor: Human Papillomavirus (HPV) Infection, Type: Infectious/Biological, Strength: Strong, Detail: Persistent infection with high-risk HPV types is the predominant pathway for vaginal SIL and SCC. HPV16 is detected in 46% to 77% of vaginal cancers. HPV is detected in approximately 40% to 70% of all vulvar and vaginal cancers [5, MS-2].
Factor: Smoking History, Type: Environmental/Lifestyle, Strength: Moderate to Strong, Detail: Epidemiologic risk factors associated with cervical cancer, including smoking, are shared risk factors for vaginal cancer [MS-2]. Smoking cessation is recommended.
Factor: Prior Hysterectomy with Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN), Type: Medical History, Strength: Strong, Detail: In individuals with cervical intraepithelial neoplasia at the time of hysterectomy, the risk of contracting vaginal cancer is more than double compared to non-hysterectomized individuals [4].
Factor: Immunosuppression, Type: Medical History, Strength: Moderate, Detail: Certain autoimmune diseases and chronic immunosuppression are shared risk factors with cervical cancer [MS-2]. HIV testing is recommended in select patients, especially younger ones [VAG-1, footnote e].
Factor: Sexual Behavior Factors, Type: Lifestyle, Strength: Moderate, Detail: Early age of onset of coitus, larger number of sexual partners, and history of sexually transmitted diseases are shared risk factors with cervical cancer [MS-2].
Factor: Obesity, Type: Lifestyle, Strength: Weak to Moderate, Detail: Obesity is listed as a risk factor based on etiologic insights from case studies [MS-2].
Factor: Vaginal Trauma or Irritation, Type: Environmental, Strength: Weak to Moderate, Detail: Risk factors include vaginal damage from ring pessaries, chronic vaginitis, birthing trauma, and exposure to chemicals in the vagina [MS-2].
Factor: Prior Pelvic Radiation, Type: Medical History/Treatment, Strength: Moderate, Detail: Patients who undergo RT for pelvic cancers are at risk for radiation-induced second cancers, including vaginal cancer [MS-11].

cards.detail: Persistent infection with high-risk HPV types is the predominant pathway for vaginal SIL and SCC. HPV16 is detected in 46% to 77% of vaginal cancers. HPV is detected in approximately 40% to 70% of all vulvar and vaginal cancers [5, MS-2].
Epidemiologic risk factors associated with cervical cancer, including smoking, are shared risk factors for vaginal cancer [MS-2]. Smoking cessation is recommended.

cards.factor: Prior Hysterectomy with Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN)
cards.detail: In individuals with cervical intraepithelial neoplasia at the time of hysterectomy, the risk of contracting vaginal cancer is more than double compared to non-hysterectomized individuals [4].

Certain autoimmune diseases and chronic immunosuppression are shared risk factors with cervical cancer [MS-2]. HIV testing is recommended in select patients, especially younger ones [VAG-1, footnote e].
Early age of onset of coitus, larger number of sexual partners, and history of sexually transmitted diseases are shared risk factors with cervical cancer [MS-2].
Obesity is listed as a risk factor based on etiologic insights from case studies [MS-2].
Risk factors include vaginal damage from ring pessaries, chronic vaginitis, birthing trauma, and exposure to chemicals in the vagina [MS-2].
Patients who undergo RT for pelvic cancers are at risk for radiation-induced second cancers, including vaginal cancer [MS-11].
